subtopic_detail: T2DM HbA1c 8.5% second-line therapy with SGLT2 inhibitor
stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 62-year-old man with a 5-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus on metformin 1 g twice daily for the past 18 months. He has stable albuminuric chronic kidney disease (eGFR 56, urine ACR 38 mg/mmol), well-controlled hypertension on perindopril, established cardiovascular disease with a previous percutaneous coronary intervention 3 years ago, and is on aspirin and high-intensity atorvastatin. His current HbA1c is 8.5% (target under 7.0%), BMI 29, blood pressure 132/82, no symptomatic hyperglycaemia. He has been adherent with metformin and has implemented dietary changes through dietitian referral for the past 6 months. He has no recent genital or urinary tract infections and no history of diabetic ketoacidosis.

data_table.Examination: No peripheral oedema, palpable foot pulses, no diabetic foot ulceration, normal cardiovascular and respiratory examination
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 8.5% (RR under 7.0% target), eGFR 56 (RR over 90), urine ACR 38 mg/mmol, LDL 1.8, BP 132/82, no acute clinical infections

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next addition to his diabetes therapy?

options.text: Commence basal insulin glargine 10 units at night and titrate by 2 units every 3 days according to fasting blood glucose with a target fasting glucose between 5 and 7 mmol/L and ongoing review of the dose with his GP
options.rationale: Insulin is generally considered after failure of oral combination therapy or when symptomatic hyperglycaemia or glucotoxicity is present; at an HbA1c of 8.5% on metformin alone the cardiorenal benefits of SGLT2 inhibition take precedence. (Source: RACGP: General practice management of T2DM)
source_refs: RACGP: General practice management of T2DM

options.text: Add gliclazide modified release 60 mg daily as second-line therapy and arrange diabetes nurse follow-up for self-monitoring of blood glucose and education about hypoglycaemia symptoms with this newly added sulfonylurea agent
options.rationale: Sulfonylureas are not first-choice second-line in a patient with established cardiovascular disease and albuminuric CKD because they provide no cardiorenal benefit and carry hypoglycaemia and weight gain risks. (Source: RACGP: General practice management of T2DM)
source_refs: RACGP: General practice management of T2DM

options.text: Add sitagliptin 100 mg daily as a DPP-4 inhibitor for its neutral cardiovascular safety profile and lack of weight gain, with monitoring for any pancreatitis-type symptoms and renal function in the months following the addition
options.rationale: DPP-4 inhibitors are weight-neutral and well tolerated but provide no cardiovascular or renal protective benefit, making them inferior to SGLT2 inhibitors in patients with established CVD or albuminuric CKD. (Source: RACGP: General practice management of T2DM)
source_refs: RACGP: General practice management of T2DM

options.text: Increase metformin to 1 g three times daily as the only intensification step at this point and review his HbA1c in 3 months before considering the addition of any second-line agent to his current diabetes regimen
options.rationale: Increasing metformin above 2 g daily yields little additional glucose-lowering and more gastrointestinal effects; he needs a second agent now and his clinical profile favours an SGLT2 inhibitor with cardiorenal benefits. (Source: RACGP: General practice management of T2DM)
source_refs: RACGP: General practice management of T2DM

options.text: Add empagliflozin 10 mg once daily as an SGLT2 inhibitor for cardiorenal protection in this man with established cardiovascular disease and albuminuric CKD, and arrange short-interval review of his BP, eGFR and ketones
options.rationale: SGLT2 inhibitors are first-line second-agent in T2DM with established cardiovascular disease or albuminuric chronic kidney disease in Australian guidance because of demonstrated cardiovascular and renal benefits independent of glycaemic effects. (Source: RACGP: General practice management of T2DM)
source_refs: RACGP: General practice management of T2DM

explanation.summary: In T2DM with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, heart failure or albuminuric chronic kidney disease, the second-line agent in Australia is an SGLT2 inhibitor (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin or ertugliflozin) independent of glycaemic control because of demonstrated cardiorenal benefit. GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, dulaglutide) are also appropriate, especially with obesity or atherosclerotic disease without heart failure. Sulfonylureas and DPP-4 inhibitors lack cardiorenal benefit. SGLT2 inhibitors are contraindicated with frequent genital infections, eGFR below threshold (varies by agent), and require sick-day rules and ketone monitoring counselling.
key_points: T2DM with CVD, HF or albuminuric CKD: SGLT2 inhibitor or GLP-1 RA as second-line, independent of glycaemic control
SGLT2 inhibitor pre-treatment: counsel on sick-day rules, genital infection risk, euglycaemic DKA, foot care
GLP-1 RA: prefer if obese or atherosclerotic disease without HF; injectable except oral semaglutide
Sulfonylureas: lower cost but hypoglycaemia and weight gain; no cardiorenal benefit
explanation.pearls: For T2DM with CVD or albuminuric CKD, the second drug after metformin is not the cheapest agent that lowers HbA1c - it is the SGLT2 inhibitor or GLP-1 agonist that will prevent his next cardiac event and slow his CKD progression.

sources.label: RACGP: General practice management of T2DM

subtopic_detail: Latent tuberculosis infection household contact management
stem: You are the infectious diseases registrar reviewing a 34-year-old asymptomatic Vietnamese-born woman who has lived in Australia for 6 months. She is a contact of her brother, who has just been diagnosed with smear-positive pulmonary tuberculosis, and they have been sharing a 2-bedroom flat together. She is well, with no cough, fever, night sweats, or weight loss. Her HIV serology is negative; she is not pregnant and is using effective contraception. Her chest x-ray is clear with no upper-lobe scarring or cavitation. A QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus assay returns positive with normal mitogen and negative nil tubes. Her routine bloods including LFTs and FBC are normal. She drinks no alcohol and takes no regular medications.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74, HR 76, T 36.8 degrees C, BMI 22, SpO2 99% in air
data_table.Examination: No respiratory or systemic symptoms, no cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy, chest clear on auscultation, no hepatomegaly
data_table.Investigations: CXR clear; QFT-Plus positive (mitogen and nil normal); HIV negative; LFTs and FBC within normal limits; sputum not produced

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Provide reassurance and arrange surveillance with annual chest x-rays for 5 years given her low individual risk of progression to active disease and the side effect burden of isoniazid in an asymptomatic young woman
options.rationale: Recent household contact of smear-positive TB confers around a 10% lifetime risk of progression (highest in the first 2 years), which clearly outweighs the side-effect burden of isoniazid in a young woman without liver disease. (Source: NTAC: Latent TB infection treatment guidance)
source_refs: NTAC: Latent TB infection treatment guidance

options.text: Repeat the QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus assay in 8 weeks and only commence treatment if the second test remains positive, given the possibility of an early false positive in a recently arrived migrant from a high-prevalence country
options.rationale: QFT-Plus has excellent specificity (around 99%) and a positive result in a household contact of smear-positive disease is acted upon directly; repeat testing delays appropriate prevention of disease progression. (Source: NTAC: Latent TB infection treatment guidance)
source_refs: NTAC: Latent TB infection treatment guidance

options.text: Commence full quadruple anti-tuberculous therapy with rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol for 2 months followed by rifampicin and isoniazid for 4 months given the high transmission risk and her recent contact
options.rationale: Quadruple RIPE therapy is for active TB; she has no symptoms, normal CXR and no microbiological evidence of active disease, so latent TB treatment is the correct intervention. (Source: NTAC: Latent TB infection treatment guidance)
source_refs: NTAC: Latent TB infection treatment guidance

options.text: Refer her to a respiratory clinic for induced sputum and bronchoalveolar lavage for tuberculosis culture and nucleic acid amplification testing before commencing latent TB therapy, with no treatment until those microbiology results are available
options.rationale: Invasive sampling is not indicated in an asymptomatic contact with a clear chest x-ray; latent TB treatment can begin once symptoms, x-ray and clinical examination exclude active disease. (Source: NTAC: Latent TB infection treatment guidance)
source_refs: NTAC: Latent TB infection treatment guidance

options.text: Commence daily oral isoniazid 5 mg/kg up to 300 mg with pyridoxine 25 mg for 6 to 9 months under the state TB service, with monthly clinical and biochemical review and confirmation of active disease exclusion before therapy commences
options.rationale: She has latent TB infection (QFT-Plus positive, asymptomatic, normal CXR) as a household contact of smear-positive pulmonary TB; Australian guidance recommends isoniazid plus pyridoxine for 6 to 9 months under specialist TB service supervision after active TB exclusion. (Source: NTAC: Latent TB infection treatment guidance)
source_refs: NTAC: Latent TB infection treatment guidance

explanation.summary: Latent TB infection in a recent household contact of smear-positive disease has approximately 10% lifetime risk of progression to active disease, with greatest risk in the first 2 years. Australian regimens for LTBI include isoniazid for 6 to 9 months with pyridoxine, or shorter rifamycin-containing regimens (rifampicin 4 months, or weekly isoniazid plus rifapentine for 12 doses where available). Active TB must be excluded clinically and radiologically before LTBI treatment begins. HIV testing and baseline LFTs are required; treatment is managed by state TB services with monthly clinical and biochemical review.
key_points: LTBI diagnosis: QFT-Plus or T-spot positive, asymptomatic, normal CXR, no microbiological evidence of active TB
Treatment options: isoniazid 6 to 9 months, rifampicin 4 months, isoniazid-rifapentine weekly for 12 doses
Pre-treatment: HIV serology, LFTs, FBC; pyridoxine with isoniazid to prevent neuropathy
Risk of progression: ~10% lifetime, highest in first 2 years and in HIV co-infection
explanation.pearls: Always pair isoniazid with pyridoxine 25 mg daily in adults - peripheral neuropathy is the dose-limiting side effect, and the relative cost of pyridoxine is so low that omitting it is never the right call.

sources.label: NTAC: Latent TB infection treatment guidance

subtopic_detail: Acute HIV seroconversion testing with 4th generation immunoassay
stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 28-year-old man with 6 days of fever, sore throat, generalised maculopapular rash, painful mouth ulcers, marked fatigue and tender cervical and inguinal lymphadenopathy. He reports condomless receptive anal intercourse with a new male partner 18 days ago. He has not been on HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis. He has had no other recent illnesses, no travel and no medications. Routine testing shows lymphopenia, transient transaminitis, and a 4th-generation HIV-1/2 antigen-antibody combination immunoassay returns positive today with a low signal-to-cutoff ratio; the supplemental Western blot is indeterminate. Syphilis serology, hepatitis B and C serology and gonorrhoea NAAT are negative. His past sexual health screening was negative 6 months ago.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/76, HR 96, T 38.4 degrees C, SpO2 99% in air
data_table.Examination: Maculopapular rash on trunk, tender cervical and inguinal lymphadenopathy, painful aphthous oral ulcers, no meningism
data_table.Investigations: 4th gen HIV Ag/Ab assay positive (low S/CO), Western blot indeterminate; lymphopenia 0.8 x10^9/L; AST 78, ALT 92; syphilis/HBV/HCV/GC negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Commence post-exposure prophylaxis with tenofovir disoproxil fumarate plus emtricitabine plus dolutegravir empirically for 4 weeks given the recent exposure 18 days ago and reassess his testing situation at the end of the prophylaxis course
options.rationale: Post-exposure prophylaxis must start within 72 hours of exposure; at 18 days post-exposure with reactive screening this is no longer PEP but established acute infection requiring HIV RNA confirmation and ART initiation. (Source: ASHM: HIV testing and management guidelines)
source_refs: ASHM: HIV testing and management guidelines

options.text: Send an HIV-1 RNA viral load assay urgently as the confirmatory test for acute HIV seroconversion, refer to a sexual health or infectious diseases physician for prompt antiretroviral therapy initiation, and arrange partner notification
options.rationale: In suspected acute seroconversion with a reactive 4th-generation Ag/Ab assay and indeterminate Western blot (typical of early seroconversion before band development), HIV RNA viral load is the appropriate confirmatory test; ART is started promptly. (Source: ASHM: HIV testing and management guidelines)
source_refs: ASHM: HIV testing and management guidelines

options.text: Order a CD4 count alone today as the confirmatory test for acute HIV infection and only commence antiretroviral therapy once the CD4 count is below 500 cells per microlitre and confirmation of seroconversion has occurred
options.rationale: CD4 count does not confirm HIV diagnosis; modern ART is started regardless of CD4 count to maximise immunological recovery, suppress viral reservoir and reduce onward transmission. (Source: ASHM: HIV testing and management guidelines)
source_refs: ASHM: HIV testing and management guidelines

options.text: Arrange supportive care alone with paracetamol and oral fluids and rebook for HIV serology in 12 weeks, since most acute seroconversion presentations are self-limiting and the diagnostic uncertainty makes ART premature at this stage of testing
options.rationale: Delaying diagnosis and ART forfeits the substantial benefit of early treatment in acute infection (reduced reservoir, lower set-point, reduced transmission); HIV RNA viral load resolves diagnostic uncertainty today. (Source: ASHM: HIV testing and management guidelines)
source_refs: ASHM: HIV testing and management guidelines

options.text: Repeat the 4th-generation HIV Ag/Ab combination immunoassay in 4 weeks before any decision about commencing antiretroviral therapy, since the present indeterminate Western blot does not confirm an HIV-positive result at the present time
options.rationale: Delaying confirmation in suspected acute seroconversion forfeits the substantial individual and public health benefit of early ART; HIV RNA viral load provides definitive same-day confirmation in this scenario. (Source: ASHM: HIV testing and management guidelines)
source_refs: ASHM: HIV testing and management guidelines

explanation.summary: Acute HIV seroconversion presents with a mononucleosis-like syndrome (fever, pharyngitis, lymphadenopathy, maculopapular rash, oral ulcers) typically 2 to 4 weeks after exposure. The 4th-generation Ag/Ab combination assay detects p24 antigen and is reactive earlier than antibody-only assays; however, the Western blot may be indeterminate in early infection before antibody bands have fully developed. HIV-1 RNA viral load is the appropriate confirmatory test (very high viral loads, often above 10^6 copies/mL in acute infection). ART is started promptly regardless of CD4 count: this reduces reservoir size, lowers set-point viral load and prevents transmission, and is referred to a sexual health or infectious diseases physician.
key_points: Acute HIV: 4th-gen Ag/Ab positive plus Western blot often indeterminate before bands develop
Confirmatory test: HIV-1 RNA viral load (typically very high in acute infection)
ART started promptly regardless of CD4 count; reduces reservoir, set-point, transmission
PEP window 72 hours post-exposure only; partner notification and contact tracing essential
explanation.pearls: An indeterminate Western blot after a reactive 4th-generation HIV assay in someone with a mononucleosis-like illness is acute seroconversion until proven otherwise - request HIV RNA the same day and start the referral to sexual health, do not wait 4 weeks to repeat serology.

sources.label: ASHM: HIV testing and management guidelines

subtopic_detail: Abdominal aortic aneurysm 5.5 cm asymptomatic surgical threshold
stem: You are the vascular surgery registrar reviewing a 72-year-old asymptomatic ex-smoker man (50 pack-year history quit 4 years ago) in clinic after a routine ultrasound surveillance scan demonstrated a 5.6 cm infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm, up from 4.8 cm 12 months ago. He has well-controlled hypertension on perindopril, an LDL of 2.0 on atorvastatin, no diabetes, no significant ischaemic heart disease on stress imaging 2 years ago, and an exercise tolerance limited only by mild osteoarthritis. He has no family history of aortic disease, no Marfan or vascular Ehlers-Danlos features, and the aneurysm morphology is suitable for endovascular repair with a long infrarenal neck of 25 mm and no significant tortuosity.

data_table.Examination: No abdominal tenderness, pulsatile non-tender central abdominal mass, palpable femoral and pedal pulses bilaterally, no distal embolic signs
data_table.Investigations: Abdominal ultrasound: infrarenal AAA 5.6 cm (was 4.8 cm 12 months ago); CT angiogram: 5.6 cm with 25 mm infrarenal neck suitable for EVAR; eGFR 62; LDL 2.0; HbA1c 5.8%

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Defer surgery and arrange repeat surveillance ultrasound in 3 months given the absence of symptoms and the satisfactory exercise tolerance, with intervention only if the aneurysm exceeds 6 cm at the next surveillance scan in 3 months
options.rationale: The Australian and international threshold for elective repair in men is 5.5 cm, not 6 cm; rapid expansion of 0.8 cm over 12 months is an independent indication for intervention. (Source: Society for Vascular Surgery and Australia and New Zealand Society for Vascular Surgery joint guidance)
source_refs: Society for Vascular Surgery and Australia and New Zealand Society for Vascular Surgery joint guidance

options.text: Continue 6-monthly ultrasound surveillance and intensify his medical therapy with the addition of a beta blocker and an angiotensin receptor blocker to slow further aneurysm expansion below the threshold for surgery in the medium term
options.rationale: At 5.6 cm with rapid expansion the annual rupture risk substantially exceeds operative mortality; deferral with intensified medical therapy is not appropriate. (Source: Society for Vascular Surgery and Australia and New Zealand Society for Vascular Surgery joint guidance)
source_refs: Society for Vascular Surgery and Australia and New Zealand Society for Vascular Surgery joint guidance

options.text: Refer for open surgical repair via midline laparotomy as the most durable repair option given his young age and good fitness, with planned admission for around 7 to 10 days of inpatient recovery and pre-operative renal optimisation
options.rationale: EVAR is preferred in patients with suitable anatomy due to lower perioperative mortality; open repair is reserved for unsuitable anatomy, younger patients with long life expectancy, or failed EVAR. (Source: Society for Vascular Surgery and Australia and New Zealand Society for Vascular Surgery joint guidance)
source_refs: Society for Vascular Surgery and Australia and New Zealand Society for Vascular Surgery joint guidance

options.text: Refer for elective endovascular aneurysm repair within the next 1 to 2 months, confirm cardiac fitness for intervention, continue strict blood pressure and lipid control and arrange smoking cessation follow-up given his history of past tobacco use
options.rationale: Surgical repair is indicated at 5.5 cm or greater in men, rapid expansion above 1 cm per year, or symptoms; he meets size, growth and morphology criteria, and EVAR is appropriate where anatomy permits and cardiac fitness allows. (Source: Society for Vascular Surgery and Australia and New Zealand Society for Vascular Surgery joint guidance)
source_refs: Society for Vascular Surgery and Australia and New Zealand Society for Vascular Surgery joint guidance

options.text: Refer for emergency endovascular aneurysm repair within the next 24 hours as the aneurysm exceeds the 5.5 cm threshold and any further delay would substantially increase his risk of imminent and catastrophic aortic rupture
options.rationale: Asymptomatic AAA above 5.5 cm without leakage is electively repaired within weeks, not as an emergency; emergency repair is for symptomatic, leaking or ruptured aneurysms. (Source: Society for Vascular Surgery and Australia and New Zealand Society for Vascular Surgery joint guidance)
source_refs: Society for Vascular Surgery and Australia and New Zealand Society for Vascular Surgery joint guidance

explanation.summary: Indications for elective AAA repair are: diameter at or above 5.5 cm in men (5.0 cm in women), rapid expansion above 1 cm per year (or above 0.5 cm in 6 months), and symptomatic disease at any size. EVAR is preferred over open repair where anatomy permits (suitable proximal neck length, angulation, iliac access), lower perioperative mortality but higher reintervention rate over time; open repair is more durable in younger fit patients. Medical management focuses on smoking cessation, BP control (avoid statin and antihypertensive omission), and lipid lowering to reduce expansion rate.
key_points: Elective repair threshold: 5.5 cm in men, 5.0 cm in women, or rapid expansion above 1 cm per year
EVAR vs open: EVAR lower 30-day mortality, higher long-term reintervention; open more durable
Suitability for EVAR depends on proximal neck length above 15 to 20 mm, angle, iliac access
Medical therapy: smoking cessation, BP control, statin, no role for routine beta-blocker for expansion
explanation.pearls: Rapid expansion above 1 cm in a year independently triggers repair even below 5.5 cm - growth rate is as important as absolute size, so always document interval growth on serial imaging rather than just the current diameter.

sources.label: Society for Vascular Surgery and Australia and New Zealand Society for Vascular Surgery joint guidance

stem: You are the endocrinology registrar reviewing a 48-year-old woman with biochemically and radiologically confirmed unilateral 4 cm right adrenal phaeochromocytoma. She presented 3 weeks ago with paroxysmal hypertension to 220/120, headache, palpitations and pallor. Investigations now show plasma free metanephrines 12 times the upper limit of normal, 24-hour urinary fractionated metanephrines 8 times normal, and a 4 cm right adrenal lesion on MRI with high T2 signal and avid uptake on 68Ga-DOTATATE PET. She is scheduled for laparoscopic right adrenalectomy in 2 weeks. She has not yet been commenced on any antihypertensive therapy. There is no family history of MEN, neurofibromatosis or von Hippel-Lindau syndrome, and genetic testing has been requested.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/108 lying, drop on standing 18 mmHg systolic; HR 96
data_table.Examination: Normal cardiovascular and respiratory examination, no carotid bruits, no abdominal mass palpable, no neurofibromas or cafe au lait macules
data_table.Investigations: Plasma free metanephrines x12 ULN, 24h urinary fractionated metanephrines x8 ULN, MRI: 4 cm right adrenal lesion high T2, 68Ga-DOTATATE PET avid; eGFR 92, K 4.0

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological management before surgery?

options.text: Commence phenoxybenzamine 10 mg twice daily titrated upward over 10 to 14 days targeting nasal stuffiness and mild postural hypotension, encourage high salt and fluid intake, then add a beta blocker only after adequate alpha blockade is achieved
options.rationale: Pre-operative non-selective alpha blockade with phenoxybenzamine for 10 to 14 days, with salt and fluid loading, followed by addition of a beta blocker only after adequate alpha blockade is the recognised sequence; starting a beta blocker before alpha blockade risks unopposed alpha effects and hypertensive crisis. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Phaeochromocytoma and paraganglioma)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Phaeochromocytoma and paraganglioma

options.text: Commence metoprolol 25 mg twice daily as initial therapy to control her resting tachycardia and to lower her blood pressure before surgery, then add an alpha blocker once her heart rate has been brought below 80 beats per minute
options.rationale: Starting a beta blocker before alpha blockade in phaeochromocytoma can precipitate hypertensive crisis through unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction; the established sequence is alpha first, then beta. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Phaeochromocytoma and paraganglioma)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Phaeochromocytoma and paraganglioma

options.text: Commence labetalol 100 mg twice daily titrated up over 7 days as a combined alpha and beta blocker as a single-agent strategy and proceed to surgery once she is normotensive on the highest tolerated dose of labetalol
options.rationale: Labetalol has relatively weak alpha-blocking activity and its mixed profile can precipitate unopposed beta effects; phenoxybenzamine (or doxazosin in some centres) followed by a separate beta blocker is preferred. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Phaeochromocytoma and paraganglioma)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Phaeochromocytoma and paraganglioma

options.text: Proceed immediately to laparoscopic right adrenalectomy without further pharmacological preparation given the moderate size of the lesion and the relatively short interval until the scheduled surgical procedure in two weeks time
options.rationale: Operating on an unblockaded phaeochromocytoma carries very high perioperative mortality from intraoperative hypertensive crisis and post-resection hypotension; 10 to 14 days of alpha blockade is mandatory. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Phaeochromocytoma and paraganglioma)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Phaeochromocytoma and paraganglioma

options.text: Commence amlodipine 5 mg daily for blood pressure control and proceed to surgery within 7 days, since calcium channel blockade alone is sufficient pre-operative preparation for laparoscopic adrenalectomy in a haemodynamically stable patient at this time
options.rationale: Calcium channel blockade does not adequately blunt the catecholamine surge of surgical manipulation; alpha blockade for 10 to 14 days with salt and fluid loading is the standard pre-operative preparation. (Source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Phaeochromocytoma and paraganglioma)
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Phaeochromocytoma and paraganglioma

explanation.summary: Pre-operative preparation for phaeochromocytoma resection requires 10 to 14 days of alpha-adrenergic blockade (phenoxybenzamine 10 mg twice daily titrated, or doxazosin 2 mg daily titrated) with salt and fluid loading to restore intravascular volume. A beta blocker is added only after adequate alpha blockade to control reflex tachycardia, never before. Adequacy markers: sitting BP under 130/80, standing systolic above 90, nasal congestion, mild postural drop. Post-resection hypotension is managed with fluids and vasopressors. Genetic testing is offered to all (at least 30% have a hereditary cause) and contralateral adrenal preservation is considered in hereditary syndromes.
key_points: Alpha blockade 10 to 14 days pre-op: phenoxybenzamine (irreversible) or doxazosin (selective alpha-1)
Beta blocker only after adequate alpha blockade; never first because of unopposed alpha vasoconstriction risk
Salt and fluid loading during alpha blockade to refill the contracted intravascular volume
Genetic testing for all (at least 30% hereditary): SDHB/C/D, RET, VHL, NF1, MAX, TMEM127
explanation.pearls: The single most preventable peri-operative phaeochromocytoma death is starting a beta blocker before alpha blockade - if a surgical team books a phaeochromocytoma case for next week and the patient is on no antihypertensives, your first prescription is phenoxybenzamine, never metoprolol or labetalol.

sources.label: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Phaeochromocytoma and paraganglioma
